mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of Linear Search in the worst case?
mcqs.explanation: In the worst case, Linear Search may need to check all n elements of the array, resulting in O(n) time complexity.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement for implementing Linear Search?
mcqs.explanation: Linear Search works on both sorted and unsorted arrays, unlike Binary Search which requires a sorted array.

mcqs.question: What is the best-case scenario for Linear Search?

options.text: Target element is at the last position
Target element is at the middle position
Target element is at the first position
Target element is not in the array

mcqs.explanation: The best case occurs when the target element is at the first position, requiring only one comparison with O(1) time complexity.

mcqs.question: In which situation would Linear Search be preferred over Binary Search?

options.text: When dealing with very large sorted arrays
When the data is frequently updated and cannot be kept sorted
When memory usage is a critical constraint
When worst-case performance is the primary concern

mcqs.explanation: Linear Search is preferred when data cannot be maintained in sorted order due to frequent updates, as Binary Search requires sorted data.

mcqs.question: What is the space complexity of the Linear Search algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: Linear Search has O(1) space complexity as it only requires a constant amount of additional space for variables like loop counters.

mcqs.question: How many comparisons does Linear Search make in the average case for an array of n elements?
mcqs.explanation: On average, Linear Search makes approximately n/2 comparisons when the target element is present in the array.

mcqs.question: Which data structure is Linear Search particularly well-suited for?
mcqs.explanation: Linear Search works well with Singly Linked Lists because it requires sequential access, which is natural for linked lists that don't support random access.

mcqs.question: What is the main advantage of the Sentinel Linear Search variation?

options.text: Reduces time complexity to O(log n)
Eliminates the need for boundary checks in the loop

mcqs.explanation: Sentinel Linear Search places the target element at the end, eliminating the need to check array boundaries in each iteration, thus reducing comparisons.

mcqs.question: In a Linear Search implementation, what should be returned if the target element is not found?
mcqs.explanation: By convention, Linear Search returns -1 to indicate that the target element was not found in the array, as array indices are non-negative.

mcqs.question: Which searching algorithm would be more efficient than Linear Search for a sorted array of 1,000,000 elements?

options.text: Only Linear Search works on such large arrays

mcqs.explanation: Binary Search with O(log n) time complexity would be significantly more efficient than Linear Search with O(n) for large sorted arrays.
